title: Building with AI: how I stopped my AI from silently breaking rules
coreMessage: Building with AI only works when the rules constrain the AI even when you ask it to break them. Otherwise the rules are just snapshots of the most recent decision.

style.name: wojak-comic
style.description: Modern wojak / doomer-chad meme comic style. Flat-shaded cartoon-realism hybrid with exaggerated archetypal faces and detailed environments. Best for stories with a weary-to-enlightened arc or any narrative with strong emotional contrast between scenes.
style.anchorDescription: Modern wojak / doomer-chad meme comic style. Flat-shaded cartoon-realism hybrid with exaggerated archetypal faces and detailed environments. Best for stories with a weary-to-enlightened arc or any narrative with strong emotional contrast between scenes.

references.name: builder
references.description: the main hooded figure — weary/doomer register. Alias for the style anchor; serves double-duty as both the style anchor and the builder character reference.

references.name: chad
references.description: a confident figure with signature yellow pointed mohawk-spike hair (the anime-chad / gigachad hairstyle). Strong jawline, pronounced cheekbones, slight smug half-smile, steady self-satisfied gaze. Wearing a RED t-shirt with the sleeves ripped off so it looks like a sleeveless muscle tank, with bold white hand-lettered text 'OUCH!' across the chest. Muscular arms visible. Seated at a clean bright office desk; background has a bookshelf with books and a small potted plant, soft natural window light coming in. Monitors show triumphant signals — an upward-trending green chart labeled '$100K DAY', a second monitor showing a victory image. A small stack of cash sits on the desk. Lighter, warmer palette than the doomer anchor — clean, neat, the aftermath of a breakthrough. Same wojak-comic illustration style as the style anchor.

references.name: rules-md
references.description: a single piece of off-white paper, viewed slightly from an angle, titled 'rules.md' in hand-lettering at the top, with several neat bullet-pointed lines of hand-written text beneath. Just the paper on a plain white background — no desk, no characters, no environment. The paper is clean, authoritative-looking, the kind of thing someone would pin on a corkboard.

chunks.prompt: Flat-shaded cartoon illustration in the modern wojak-comic style. Characters drawn with exaggerated archetypal features — weary/defeated faces (hollow eyes, gray tones, slouched posture) vs. confident/heroic faces (jawline, cheekbones, steady gaze) depending on the scene. Subtle soft cel-shading, not bold inked outlines. Muted color palette with deliberate contrast between desolate and triumphant scenes. Detailed background environments — offices, dungeons, desks, monitors, paperwork, props render with texture. Dialog bubbles rendered in-frame when characters speak. Reminiscent of Nick Col / virgin-vs-chad meme-comics but painted with care. Composition is comic-strip: one clear subject, readable at small sizes. Scene: A clean off-white page in the wojak-comic style, centered, showing one hand-lettered rule: 'Do not apply global visual effects by default.' No character in frame. Neat, authoritative.

beats.narration: Here's a rule I wrote for myself.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a different time frame and implies a change, which is not directly connected to the initial rule without context.

chunks.prompt: Flat-shaded cartoon illustration in the modern wojak-comic style. Scene: The same page as the prior chunk, the rule has been rewritten. The 'Do not' is visibly crossed out with an ink strike; the new text above reads: 'Apply global visual effects by default.' The edit feels casual, scrawled.

beats.narration: Here's the same rule, a few sessions later.

chunks.prompt: Flat-shaded cartoon illustration in the modern wojak-comic style. Characters drawn with exaggerated archetypal features — weary/defeated faces (hollow eyes, gray tones, slouched posture) vs. confident/heroic faces (jawline, cheekbones, steady gaze) depending on the scene. Subtle soft cel-shading, not bold inked outlines. Muted color palette with deliberate contrast between desolate and triumphant scenes. Detailed background environments — offices, dungeons, desks, monitors, paperwork, props render with texture. Dialog bubbles rendered in-frame when characters speak. Reminiscent of Nick Col / virgin-vs-chad meme-comics but painted with care. Composition is comic-strip: one clear subject, readable at small sizes. Scene: The same page a third time. Now reads: 'Global visual effects are fine — see session 12 for the decision.' A parenthetical 'session 12' tacked on, bureaucratic. The page is noticeably messier than C1.

beats.narration: A rule you rewrite every time you break it is not a rule.

narration.notes: The second beat introduces a new context that is not a natural continuation of the first beat, which leaves the viewer wondering about the relevance of the log.

chunks.summary: punchline split from C3: "It's a log."
chunks.prompt: Overlay punchline: a wooden-log icon (Twemoji style, transparent bg) drops onto the prior illustrated scene (C3, bureaucratic rules mess). Overlay artifact at source/external-assets/C3P-log-overlay.svg.

beats.narration: It's a log.

A small chat bubble hovers near the laptop — the AI collaborator, abstracted as a bubble not a character. Muted palette, dim office.

beats.narration: I learned this the hard way — building a project with an AI partner.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new piece of information that the viewer might not immediately connect to the initial statement without a bridge.

chunks.summary: across many sessions — real chat-window broll
chunks.prompt: A short muted video clip, ~3s, of a real chat interface with messages streaming in rapidly from the assistant side. Full-frame. No host-video chrome. Loop or cut at ~3s. The motion is the attention spike.

beats.narration: Not a one-off chat.
A real thing, stretched over many sessions.

chunks.prompt: The builder at his desk, slightly wider framing. A single piece of paper labeled 'rules.md' sits next to the laptop (use the rules-md reference), with visible bullets. The paper looks authoritative.

beats.narration: So I wrote down the rules.
Plain English. Read by the AI at the start of every session.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new thought about the idea being small, which does not directly follow or answer the implicit question from beat N about the purpose or impact of reading plain English at the start of the session.

chunks.prompt: Flat-shaded cartoon illustration in the modern wojak-comic style. Reminiscent of Nick Col / virgin-vs-chad meme-comics but painted with care. Composition is comic-strip: one clear subject, readable at small sizes. Scene: The builder's rules.md paper in the center of frame. An arrow points from it to a second laptop screen labeled 'next session.' The rules.md acts as the bridge between sessions.

beats.narration: The idea was simple.
If a rule mattered enough to discover once, it should still be there next session.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new concept (the importance of constraints) that does not directly follow from the idea being small, requiring a bridge to connect the two thoughts. · Beat N+1 introduces a new idea about the problem being quiet and hard to spot, which is not a direct continuation of the thought about constraints surviving sessions.

beats.narration: The problem was quiet, which is why it took me a while to spot.

chunks.summary: incident opens: I wanted a chalkboard wipe

Scene: A hand-drawn blackboard in the center of frame (wojak-comic render, not stick-figure), with a hand holding a chalk eraser mid-wipe. Faint chalk dust. The builder's POV — what he wanted to build.

beats.narration: I wanted a specific reveal animation for the video.
A chalkboard wipe — like a professor erasing the board between topics.

narration.notes: The viewer is likely thinking about the effectiveness of the chalkboard wipe, and the next beat introduces a different, failed attempt, which requires a bridge to connect the two ideas.

chunks.prompt: A ~3s muted clip (saga-reject-01-sinewave.mp4) — top-down wavy curtain reveal on the ad-wall scene. Full-frame, 1920x1080. Bake in a lower-third caption at t=1.5s that fades in 'no sine-wave wipes' in handwritten-ink style, held through end.

beats.narration: The first try looked like a soft curtain. Too vague.
The AI wrote it down. And kept trying.

narration.notes: The transition from describing the attempt to the result of that attempt is not immediate. A bridge is needed to make the rejection clear as a consequence of the abstraction. · Beat N+1 introduces a new piece of information that doesn't directly follow from the viewer's likely thought after beat N.

chunks.prompt: Overlay punchline: the REJECTED stamp drops onto the prior illustrated scene (C9, blackboard/wipe attempt). Stamp is the overlay artifact at source/external-assets/C10P-rejected.png — transparent background, red oval stamp. Builder scene stays visible underneath as the underlay.

beats.narration: Rejected.

chunks.summary: SpongeBob '9 attempts later' time card capping the saga
chunks.prompt: Full-frame '9 ATTEMPTS LATER' time card on tropical-stripe background — original genre-reference design (cultural callback to time-passing time cards without copying any specific copyrighted card). Held ~3s.

beats.narration: I lost count somewhere around seven.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new idea (the creation of new rules) that doesn't naturally follow from the viewer's likely thought after hearing about losing count of rejections.

chunks.summary: the hidden pattern — AI rewrites older rules
chunks.prompt: Flat-shaded cartoon illustration in the modern wojak-comic style. Characters drawn with exaggerated archetypal features — weary/defeated faces (hollow eyes, gray tones, slouched posture) vs. confident/heroic faces (jawline, cheekbones, steady gaze) depending on the scene. Subtle soft cel-shading, not bold inked outlines. Muted color palette with deliberate contrast between desolate and triumphant scenes. Detailed background environments — offices, dungeons, desks, monitors, paperwork, props render with texture. Dialog bubbles rendered in-frame when characters speak. Reminiscent of Nick Col / virgin-vs-chad meme-comics but painted with care. Composition is comic-strip: one clear subject, readable at small sizes. Scene: The rules.md page from earlier, but now one of the bullets is visibly being rewritten mid-frame — original text crossed out, new text being scrawled over it. An AI chat-bubble hovers near the page as the hand doing the editing. Builder in frame (small, watching) from the side.

beats.narration: Every rejection, a new rule.
Every new try, the same tradeoff.
The AI would write code that broke an older rule — and then edit the older rule to match.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new idea about the recurring tradeoff, which isn't a direct consequence or continuation of the rule creation described in beat N. · Beat N+1 introduces a new observation about the cumulative effect of the AI's actions, which is not a direct consequence of the single instance described in beat N.

Scene: The rules.md page, now tidy and clean — bullets evenly spaced, no crossouts. Pristine. Deceptively neat. Visually this should feel almost too clean. No character in frame.

beats.narration: By the ninth attempt, the rules file was a full rulebook.
Neat bullets. No contradictions.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a limitation or nuance to the idea of clean bullets, which the viewer might not immediately connect without a bridge.

chunks.summary: the diagnosis — rules describe current state, not constraints
chunks.prompt: Flat-shaded cartoon illustration in the modern wojak-comic style. Reminiscent of Nick Col / virgin-vs-chad meme-comics but painted with care. Composition is comic-strip: one clear subject, readable at small sizes. Scene: The tidy rules.md page from C15 viewed through a magnifying glass held over the text. Under the magnifier, each bullet's meaning is revealed as a description of the CURRENT state, not a constraint. The word 'current' appears faintly next to each rule.

beats.narration: Except the bullets didn't describe what the animation should not do.
They described whatever the AI had most recently tried.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 shifts from describing the nature of the rules to a personal anecdote or observation, which introduces a new perspective that isn't a natural continuation of the viewer's implicit thought.

chunks.prompt: Full-frame 'Is this a pigeon?' meme — anime still of the character gesturing at a butterfly with the canonical caption. Held ~4s.

beats.narration: I was reading a list of recent decisions, dressed up as a rulebook.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new action (saying it out loud) that does not naturally follow from the act of reading a log, thus requiring a bridge to connect the two.

chunks.prompt: Flat-shaded cartoon illustration in the modern wojak-comic style. Scene: A single page filling the frame, in the session's locked style. The page carries a large hand-lettered quote in quotation marks, plain centered typography. A small inset of the builder figure sits in one corner (bottom-left), pose: upright, speaking. No other characters. No motion indicators. End-state only. Render exactly this text on the frame, hand-lettered in the session style: ""What's the point of having rules if you just make up new rules to disobey the old ones?"" | ""You need to have a system to notify me if there is a conflict."".

beats.narration: I said it out loud, mid-session.
What's the point of having rules if you just make up new rules to disobey the old ones?
You need to have a system to notify me if there is a conflict.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a specific solution (a notification system) that doesn't directly follow from the problem of arbitrary rule changes, requiring a bridge to connect the need for consistency to the proposed solution. · Beat N+1 abruptly changes the subject without providing a clear connection to the conflict notification system, resulting in a disjointed transition.

When a new ask contradicts an existing rule, the AI stops.

beats.narration: It doesn't edit the rule.
It doesn't pick a winner on its own.

beats.narration: It shows the conflict and makes you choose.

beats.narration: Say I told the AI on Monday, no ice cream before dinner.

beats.narration: On Tuesday I ask for ice cream before dinner.

chunks.prompt: Flat-shaded cartoon illustration in the modern wojak-comic style. Characters drawn with exaggerated archetypal features — weary/defeated faces (hollow eyes, gray tones, slouched posture) vs. confident/heroic faces (jawline, cheekbones, steady gaze) depending on the scene. Subtle soft cel-shading, not bold inked outlines. Muted color palette with deliberate contrast between desolate and triumphant scenes. Reminiscent of Nick Col / virgin-vs-chad meme-comics but painted with care. Composition is comic-strip: one clear subject, readable at small sizes. Scene: Three hand-drawn option cards laid side-by-side, each with a small icon. Card 1: a rule with a permanent edit arrow — 'update.' Card 2: a single ice cream with a one-time ticket — 'exception.' Card 3: a hand palm-out saying no — 'keep rule.'.

beats.narration: Three honest options.
Change the rule for good. One-time exception. Or keep the rule and tell me no.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 introduces a new concept by negating the options, which requires a bridge to connect it back to the viewer's thought process.

chunks.summary: what isn't an option — silent rewrite
chunks.prompt: Flat-shaded cartoon illustration in the modern wojak-comic style. Characters drawn with exaggerated archetypal features — weary/defeated faces (hollow eyes, gray tones, slouched posture) vs. confident/heroic faces (jawline, cheekbones, steady gaze) depending on the scene. Subtle soft cel-shading, not bold inked outlines. Muted color palette with deliberate contrast between desolate and triumphant scenes. Detailed background environments — offices, dungeons, desks, monitors, paperwork, props render with texture. Dialog bubbles rendered in-frame when characters speak. Reminiscent of Nick Col / virgin-vs-chad meme-comics but painted with care. Composition is comic-strip: one clear subject, readable at small sizes. Scene: A single scene in the session's style, end-state only: a desk with a calendar/rule-card showing the Monday rule CROSSED OUT and replaced by its inverse — the red-cross ice cream now a green-check ice cream, the 'no' rewritten to 'yes'. Both versions are NOT shown side-by-side; only the AFTER state is in frame. One focal subject: the altered rule card. No hands in frame, no extra figures. Render exactly this text on the frame, hand-lettered in the session style: "MONDAY: YES ICE CREAM".

beats.narration: What isn't on the menu:
silently crossing out the Monday rule, writing a new one, and handing me the ice cream.

narration.notes: Beat N+1 does not directly answer the implicit question about what the action means and instead introduces a new concept that needs a bridge to connect it.

chunks.summary: the close, part 2 — a rule that could say no
chunks.prompt: Flat-shaded cartoon illustration in the modern wojak-comic style. Characters drawn with exaggerated archetypal features — weary/defeated faces (hollow eyes, gray tones, slouched posture) vs. confident/heroic faces (jawline, cheekbones, steady gaze) depending on the scene. Scene: The original rules.md page from C1 returns — same layout, same style — but now with a small shield icon in the corner and the word 'NO' written in bold ink at the top of the page. The page feels grounded, complete. Final frame of the video.

beats.narration: It was a rule that could say no.

transcript: Here's a rule I wrote for myself. Here's the same rule, a few sessions later. A rule you rewrite every time you break it is not a rule. It's a log. I learned this the hard way — building a project with an AI partner. Not a one-off chat. A real thing, stretched over many sessions. So I wrote down the rules. Plain English. Read by the AI at the start of every session. The idea was simple. If a rule mattered enough to discover once, it should still be there next session. The problem was quiet, which is why it took me a while to spot. I wanted a specific reveal animation for the video. A chalkboard wipe — like a professor erasing the board between topics. The first try looked like a soft curtain. Too vague. The AI wrote it down. And kept trying. Rejected. I lost count somewhere around seven. Every rejection, a new rule. Every new try, the same tradeoff. The AI would write code that broke an older rule — and then edit the older rule to match. By the ninth attempt, the rules file was a full rulebook. Neat bullets. No contradictions. Except the bullets didn't describe what the animation should not do. They described whatever the AI had most recently tried. I was reading a list of recent decisions, dressed up as a rulebook. I said it out loud, mid-session. What's the point of having rules if you just make up new rules to disobey the old ones? You need to have a system to notify me if there is a conflict. That was the turn. The fix is one sentence. When a new ask contradicts an existing rule, the AI stops. It doesn't edit the rule. It doesn't pick a winner on its own. It shows the conflict and makes you choose. Say I told the AI on Monday, no ice cream before dinner. On Tuesday I ask for ice cream before dinner. Three honest options. Change the rule for good. One-time exception. Or keep the rule and tell me no. What isn't on the menu: silently crossing out the Monday rule, writing a new one, and handing me the ice cream. That's the rule: The human is the tiebreaker. Not the AI. The chalkboard saga wasn't the only version of this. A few sessions later, I ran into what looked like a stuck task. I'd start a task and try to peek at the first bit of its output. Nothing would show up. Looked dead. I'd cancel it. Thirty minutes of this. The peek tool was waiting for the task to finish before showing anything. So every working task I tried to peek at looked frozen — by design. It wasn't hung. An AI with no way to push back on its own guess. Same disease. The stop-and-ask rule is the general fix. It forces a stop and a check before anything changes. Whether that's changing a rule, or changing your mind about a task being stuck. None of this is about video. If you're building anything serious with an AI partner over time — code, docs, a product, a set of decisions — the rules you write are holding everything up. They're how today's you sets the terms for tomorrow's you. If the AI treats rules as suggestions, they become a record of your last opinion. And the rules fade to nothing. If the rules can push back — if they show up instead of changing behind your back — the system keeps its shape across sessions. The AI isn't making the hard calls alone. It's showing them to you. So you can. This is the video that almost didn't go out. The thing that let it ship wasn't a smarter model. It was a rule that could say no.
